column1: Mortality estimates for years up to 2005 are based on Australian Bureau of Statistics death registration data. Data from 2006 onwards were provided by the Australian Coordinating Registry, Cause of Death Unit Record File; the data for the 2 most recent years are preliminary, while the year prior to that is revised data. (SAPHaRI, Centre for Epidemiology and Evidence, NSW Ministry of Health). Population denominators for rate calculations are based on estimates and projections from ABS estimated resident populations, NSW Department of Planning, Housing and Infrastructure, and Aboriginal Affairs NSW.

Centre for Epidemiology and Evidence.  HealthStats NSW. Sydney:  NSW Ministry of Health Available at: https://www.healthstats.nsw.gov.au/r/133897 Accessed: Sat Apr 18 2026 18:02:46 GMT+1000 (Australian Eastern Standard Time)
Suicide comprises any intentional self-harm with fatal result. Intentional self harm includes suicide (attempted) and purposely self-inflicted poisoning or injury (WHO 1992).

The cause of death unit record file is a snapshot of data that is compiled and coded (ICD-10) by the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS), based on data from the data custodians, that is correct as at a point in time.

The ABS undertake a revision process for coroner-certified deaths over a 3-year period. Accordingly, causes of death data for the two most recent years reported are preliminary and subject to two further revisions; data for the third most recent year are revised and subject to another revision; data for earlier years are final.
Deaths were classified using ICD-10 from 1997 onwards.
Counts of deaths for the latest year of data include an estimate of the number of deaths occurring in that year but registered in the next year.
Rates were age-adjusted using the Australian population as at 30 June 2001.

Age standardised rates (also known as age-adjusted rates) apply an age adjustment to the rates of health outcomes (usually via a standardised population) so that comparison can be made across sub-populations, diseases or time. Age standardisation is required as age structures differ across time and between sub-populations, and disease rates generally differ across the different age groups.
95% confidence intervals were calculated using the Gamma method.

The NSW Aboriginal populations used for direct age standardisation in HealthStats NSW are based on contemporaneous census estimates for 2011, 2016, and 2021 published by the ABS; population projections published by Aboriginal Affairs NSW; and linear interpolation for years in between. The non-Aboriginal populations for comparison are estimated by subtracting Aboriginal population estimates from the estimated total population for NSW.

For NSW deaths registered in 2022 onwards, the ABS has access to Aboriginal status information from the Medical Certificate Cause of Death (MCCD) form and from the National Coronial Information System (NCIS) for Coroner-certified deaths, in addition to information reported on the Death Registration Statement (DRS). Data prior to 2022 reflects under-reporting of Aboriginal deaths in NSW.These changes created a break in time series in Aboriginal mortality statistics in NSW and Australia. Trends spanning periods before and after 2022 cannot be accurately assessed, unless deaths records for Aboriginal people in NSW prior to 2022 are updated using information available in MCCD and Coronial Information Systems. Historical data prior to 2022 reflects under-reporting of Aboriginal deaths in NSW.
As Aboriginal people are under-reported on Death Registration Forms, it is likely that the true numbers are higher than shown.

Local Health Districts (LHDs) are health administrative areas constituted under Section 17 of the NSW Health Services Act 1997 which became effective from January 2011 and were initially called Local Health Networks.
Murrumbidgee LHD excludes Albury Local Government Area (LGA).
Data for Far West LHD has been suppressed due to small numbers, and Western NSW LHD has been suppressed to prevent potential back-calculation of suppressed values.
All LHDs includes LHDs where numbers are too low to be individually included, and records where the LHD was missing or not stated.
Primary Health Networks (PHNs) are health administrative areas which represent primary health care organisations in Australia from July 2014.
All PHNs include records where the Primary Health Network is missing or not stated as well as records assigned to the NSW portion of the Murray PHN.
The Accessibility/Remoteness Index of Australia Plus (ARIA plus) is a remoteness index value (or score) based on road distance to major service centres (GISCA).
Statistical Areas are grouped according to Australian Statistical Geographic Standard (ASGS) remoteness categories on the basis of Accessibility/Remoteness Index for Australia (ARIA version) score.
Outer regional and remote includes very remote.

Socio-Economic Indexes for Areas (SEIFA) are produced by the Australian Bureau of Statistics, with four indexes currently being produced. Index of Relative Socio-Economic Disadvantage (IRSD) is the main index reported by HealthStats NSW. It includes characteristics relating to occupation, education, non-English speaking background and the economic resources of the household.

Quintiles of socioeconomic status (Index of Relative Socioeconomic Disadvantage) based on the Australian Bureau of Statistics' Socio-Economic Indexes for Areas were allocated based on Statistical Local Area of residence (before 2009) or Statistical Area Level 2 of residence (2009 and after).
Regional health districts in NSW are defined on the basis of Local Health District (LHD) geographic areas. Metropolitan and regional health districts are defined here.
